summary: Joel Spolsky shares his frustrations from reviewing hundreds of internship applications at Fog Creek Software, most of which contain easily avoidable mistakes. He catalogs common errors including poor formatting, generic cover letters, failure to follow application instructions, and lack of basic professionalism. He then offers constructive advice: proofread obsessively, write personalized cover letters, follow directions exactly, and don't mass-apply. His core message is that a résumé's job is to avoid elimination, not to win the job, and the best way to stand out is to come across as a genuine human being rather than a list of skills.
key_points: A résumé's primary function is to avoid giving the hiring manager a reason to reject you, not to actively win you the job
Generic cover letters, formatting errors, and failure to follow application instructions are immediate disqualifiers when competition is fierce
Personalizing your application to show you understand the specific company matters far more than mass-applying to hundreds of positions
The most effective résumé strategy is to come across as a human being with a story, not a sterile list of technologies and job titles
When reviewing 300 applicants for 2 positions, superficial mistakes become legitimate screening criteria because there are always enough polished candidates
money_quotes: Don't think of a résumé as a way to get a job: think of it as a way to give some hiring manager an excuse to hit DELETE.
The scanner is right next to the shredder in my office and the shredder is easier to use.

If you write 'I understand the position also requires a candidate who is team- and detail-oriented, works well under pressure' then at best people will think you're a bullshit artist and at worst they will think that you were not born with the part of the brain that allows you to form your own thoughts and ideas.
The number one best way to get someone to look at your resume closely: come across as a human being, not a list of jobs and programming languages.
I just can't care about 'C/C++/Perl/ASP' in the same way.
Résuméspam, or any sign that you're applying for 100 jobs, just makes you look desperate which makes you look unqualified.
Much as I'd love to be able to consider everyone on their merits instead of on superficial résumé stuff, it's just not realistic, and there's just no reason a college graduate can't get this right.
I like you. I care about you. I like the fact that you want to work in a real software company. I wanted to work in a real software company so much I started one.
key_insight: In a competitive hiring process, your résumé's job is not to prove you're qualified but to avoid giving the reviewer any excuse to reject you — and the best way to survive that filter is to come across as a genuine human being.
